extraction_metadata.method: Extracted from PubMed MCP full text — real paper
extraction_metadata.confidence_note: Full text was very detailed. Tables and figures referenced but not rendered in XML. However, the paper text contains substantial quantitative information, method details, and model parameters. This is the most comprehensive CCR/CWR paper in the field.

paper.title: Effect of Common Cryoprotectants on Critical Warming Rates and Ice Formation in Aqueous Solutions
affiliations: Physics Department, Cornell University, Ithaca, NY

experimental_methods.ice_detection: visual assay (transparency/opacity) + high-speed camera (up to 1200 fps)
experimental_methods.rate_definition: average rate between 150K (-123°C) and 240K (-33°C) — roughly Tg of water to Thom of water

cwr_vs_ccr_ratio.description: CWR is uniformly 10-1000x larger than CCR at the same concentration for ALL cryoprotectants studied

concentration_dependence.equation: CCR or CWR = C0 * exp(-alpha * c)
concentration_dependence.description: Both CCR and CWR show approximately exponential dependence on concentration (w/w) for all CPAs
concentration_dependence.note: alpha is larger for warming than cooling (by factor 1.2 to 3.2)

cooling_rate_effect_on_cwr.finding: Increasing cooling rate from ~20x CCR to ~200x CCR decreased CWR by factor of at least 15
cooling_rate_effect_on_cwr.specific_data: At cooling rate ~1000 K/s (~200x CCR), CWR dropped to ~350 K/s
cooling_rate_effect_on_cwr.implication: Samples should ALWAYS be cooled at maximum possible rate to minimize required warming rate

glycerol_model_validation.finding: Model reasonably predicts CWR at high concentrations/low rates, but overestimates both CCR and CWR by orders of magnitude at lower concentrations/larger rates

data_tables_referenced_but_not_rendered.description: Measured critical warming rates for glycerol, DMSO, ethylene glycol, PEG 200, sucrose — with cooling rates, sample volumes, and warming rates
Parameters from exponential fits: C0 and alpha for CCR and CWR for each CPA
Measured critical cooling rates for DMSO, EG, PEG 200
Semi-log plots of CWR and CCR vs concentration for all 5 CPAs — THE KEY DATA FIGURE
CWR vs cooling rate for 44% w/w ethylene glycol
Model predictions vs experimental data for glycerol

scientific_insights: Warming is MORE important than cooling for sample survival — ice forms much more readily on warming
CWR is always 10-1000x higher than CCR for the same solution
Both CCR and CWR decrease exponentially with CPA concentration
CWR strongly depends on cooling rate — faster cooling → lower CWR needed
At large cooling/warming rates (relevant to vitrification), homogeneous nucleation dominates over heterogeneous
Classical nucleation models fail at low concentrations and high rates — orders of magnitude off
CPA-containing cellular solutions may have similar CWR/CCR to cell-free solutions at high rates
Minimizing CPA concentration (reducing toxicity) requires ultrafast warming rates

formulation_data_from_text.note: The actual CWR/CCR values at specific concentrations are in Tables 1-3 and Figures 2-5 (not rendered). Below are values extractable from the body text.

critical_hackathon_note: THIS PAPER IS THE SINGLE MOST VALUABLE DATA SOURCE FOR THE PROJECT. Tables 1-3 contain ~80-130 individual CCR/CWR measurements across 5 CPAs at multiple concentrations. The PMC full text does NOT render these tables. At the hackathon, processing the PDF directly via Claude API will capture all table data. This one paper alone could provide enough data for the predictive model.
